speaker: interviewer
text: 23rd of November, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't-- at which point was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with your brother in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when there was the [NON-ENGLISH], action, what-- which means that they wanted to lower the number of living Jews, to put it mildly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they would come in one morning with-- in their cars, in-- on horses, and-- mounted on horses. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were so many of them. And suddenly we have to go to the [? Arbeitsamt, ?] which is the employment office. And there, they will give us new [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] papers to enable us to stay in the ghetto, or simply go-- be taken, in other way-- in other words. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one morning, we got up, and there it is. Thousands of peoples in front of that employment office. I was with my sister. But at that time, my brother slept-- he worked in a hospital, and so he slept in this hospital, because the room was so little that if the three of us were together in the room, my sister and I slept in the bed, and my brother had to sleep in the-- in a sort of like a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in a chair, we-- rocking chair, sort of. It wasn't a rocking chair. Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so, because he slept in the hospital, I said to my sister, let's go and get the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thousands upon thousands of people. But then again, you knew that one and that one who kept order, and Jews. Not Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were the Jewish police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish police, but they were-- I knew them from before the war. Two of them were pretty Orthodox, too. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got there, and the man to see was-- Schepessy was his name, a German in a uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he from the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I got the necessary paper. And I got it for my sister, like sort of being with me. No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't get it for her, because she wasn't of age, you see. So she went wherever one of us went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when I-- when I got it, I took my sister, and I said, let's go and see whether somebody has seen Beno. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Among thousands of people, how do you find out whether someone has seen Beno? And I was asking here, there, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a matter of fact, I have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of stumbled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] into a person who was my sister-in-law's brother-in-law. Not at that time, not in the ghetto. It became like that after the war. And I said, listen, have you seen my brother? They knew each other. And he says, I think they took him to Optima. Optima was a chocolate-- chocolate factory before the war. And it had an enormous yard, as you can imagine. And they took all the Jews that they wanted to exterminate to that yard. When I heard that, my heart nearly stopped beating. And I said to my sister, Eda, we either go together or die together. I mean, or live together. But how do I get there? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, I sneaked myself between with my sister, and went there, with the [NON-ENGLISH], with the [NON-ENGLISH] in my pocket. When I came there, among so many thousands, I was looking around. We were looking. And we didn't part with my-- with my sister, because I was afraid we would lose each other. Well, we went first home. That's what we called it, home. I took blankets wherever we go, because at that time, you must realize, we didn't know that they are killing us. That-- we didn't know about Auschwitz. We didn't know any of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we thought, well, wherever we go, we'll need a blanket. And I was just walking around and looking and looking, and crying and looking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother was sitting just like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, hi. He said, what are you doing here? And I said, same you do here. What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I didn't get a [NON-ENGLISH]. So I said, well, I didn't either. So here we are, the three of us. And then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then-- but it was all a mixed bunch, men and women and children and everybody. And I said, Beno, there must be a way out of here. And please, forget what you think will happen. Just hope for the best. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, then they separated the men from the women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Children went with the women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I just kept my eye where my brother is, so that I wouldn't have to search for him in case we have to say goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] suddenly, I saw a limousine coming. Some officers sitting, and one was standing up. Were they Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were plenty of them there. But I thought, ah, the car, the-- the passes, the letters from [INAUDIBLE], that would come handy. And I-- I have-- by nature, I think, can I do something about it? And I try. And if I say, I can't do anything about it, then I forget it. In a situation like this, you have to be very quick. And I said, I'm going there. I'll talk to him. And I said to my sister, you stay still here. And as I left [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I was standing, from where I was standing, I ran, and they started shooting. So I thought, the car goes that way. If I go the same way, they can't shoot, because they will shoot the Ger-- standing up German too. So that's the only way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was running parallel with the car, until the Germans, the Na-- the SS men were running after me, came over, because the car wasn't running-- going at a speed. It was very slowly. It wasn't on a highway. And he sort of looked at me, and he said to the driver, in German language, will you stop. What is that nuisance here? And he stopped. And he asked me, and I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think there is-- there has a mistake been made. Of course, there are so many of us, I said, so that's possible. You had to be terribly careful what you are saying. And-- in German-- and I said, you see, I work for the-- for a factory which makes nets for the Luftwaffe. And if I am not there, that's a very specialized thing. Who will make-- who will produce it? Oh, show-- show me. And I did. So he said-- he called a-- a Jewish policeman and said, take her back to the ghetto. I didn't tell him that I had the [NON-ENGLISH] anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I said, but my brother, who is the manager, is here too. Without him, we-- I can't do anything, because he makes the machines going. And I went to the-- to my brother, which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a fair run, but I was running. I wasn't walking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, you say that we are working. And he said to me, I'm not going to lie. And I said, for heaven's sake, who are you cheating? I am not a liar. So I said, OK. Come with me. So in order not to put my brother in that situation that he-- he wouldn't lie. He just wouldn't. So when he asked him, what do you do there? And I said, as I said, he is the manager. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he said, OK. He said to that Jewish policeman. And he said, take them back to the ghetto. And I said, what about my sister? She works there too. She's only very young, but she works. And it's a small staff there, because the [NON-ENGLISH] doesn't want to employ more people. So we have to do with whatever we can-- work hard and-- so that the Luftwaffe can operate. How many bloody Jews is still left in your family? And I said, none. What happened to them? Oh, well, we were sort of orphans. Because you can't say the Ger-- Nazis have killed her. When we walked back, my brother said to me, if I wouldn't know you, I would have thought that you are a born liar. And I said, for heaven's sake. Your life is at stake. You don't-- you cheat your enemy. What's war about? It's cheating your enemy. That's how you can win. Well, we got to the ghetto, only to be told to pick up the corpses that in the meantime were lying in the streets. I've got the books with pictures. How long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: To 1943. And then we were taken-- first it was a [NON-ENGLISH] Arbeitslager, which is a compulsory work camp-- Plaszow. Then it was turned into a [NON-ENGLISH] Konzentrationslager, which didn't make much difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, it's just in the name, because it was the most shocking place one can imagine. We were there, and the commandant-- commandant? Is this in English? He was a-- an animal. Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goeth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After the war, they caught him. They tried him in Cracow, and he was hanged in Cracow. Of this I have a picture too. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] An animal without any-- I mean, you can see reasonable animals. I mean, they shoot, they kill. But this one was deriving some special pleasure out of it. He could come out on a horse, having all of us lined up-- 20,000 of us-- and he will make a bet with another animal, like himself, that with one bullet he will kill 10 Jews. Line up one next-- after the other and shoot him-- shoot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You never knew which one would be picked. One day it was one every fifth. Once it was one every tenth. It-- it just-- I-- we had times where we had to stand naked. We used to call it the naked parade. Without any reason whatsoever, they would line us up, call us for the-- call roll. Is that how you say it? Roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: Roll call. Sorry. And naked, just as you were born. And stand in the snow or run in the snow for hours upon hours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once, I remember a young boy, because they counted us a few times a day. And they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one was missing, a young boy. I don't know. He could have been 16, 17. He tried to run away. So we were standing there on-- in the snow, I think, a day and night. And next morning, they caught him. They brought him. They hung him. And I'll never forget when they hung him, before they, you know, hung him. And he knew there's no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] way out of it. And he was calling out, don't give up. And he was started singing the Hatikvah. But he hasn't finished singing, because they killed him-- hung him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from there, we were taken every day for a few months-- few weeks, few months, I can't remember-- to Zablocie camp. That was Schindler's, Oskar Schindler, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who I think would be known to everybody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were escorted in the morning and escorted back to the Plaszow camp in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who escorted you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The men in the black uniforms, who were mostly Ukrainians, Latvians, and Lithuanians. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And one day, Schindler decided that he wants to build a camp there. And so he built-- he has built a few barracks. And we stayed there. They didn't-- we were not going back-- backwards and forwards to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become one of the people who stayed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a man called Mr. Bankier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a factory with-- an enamel factory, pots and pans and what not. And as I mentioned before, every business was allocated a [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] of his business was Mr. Schindler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started talking and talking, and he met another Jew and another Jew through that Bankier-- Mr. Bankier. And he said, hey, listen, we might as well make-- make something out of it. Bring some workers. You got family? Yes. Well, he brought his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the family had some family. And the family had some friends. And the friend had a friend. And this friend had their relation. So it has built up to 300 women and 600 men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my husband, Peter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] knew someone that knew that-- the way there. I don't know. Anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back to your husband for a moment, when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, July, I presume. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue with Plaszow, can you just tell me a little bit about how you got married in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were catching people in the ghetto from the streets to go to Wieliczka, which is a little town about 80 kilometers from Cracow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- where they had a little ghetto. When they took the Jews away from that ghetto, they were catching us-- they caught us to this place, Wieliczka, put us in a building-- two buildings, men in one and girls in the other. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to clean up the ghetto. It was heartbreaking, because you walked into a place. You could see that the people were just eating a meal, but they left everything and they were taken away. And so we were cleaning up this. We were taking furniture to some storage. And of course, we had to carry it on our backs. But at that time, that wasn't a concentration camp. It was just a working camp. We were-- we were guarded, yes. But I must mention-- this is a very interesting thing. I was working, carrying typewriters, desks, with another girl. And there was always a man there, in civilian clothes, watching me. And when the Wehrmacht-- the soldiers-- when he turned around, this man always put in my hand something. Well, I couldn't open it up. I couldn't talk to him. We were not allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I got back to the camp, it was a per-- bottle with perfume. Next time, it was a little bit bigger. Something else was with something else. I didn't know what-- who the man was. Very elegant and very handsome. And I thought, God, who is this? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just didn't trust, you know? We have learned not to trust. And one day, he-- once he brought me a cake. And how do you carry a cake so that the Wehrmacht men wouldn't see? Well, put under-- you learn the tricks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it-- it puzzled me. It really did, because I wouldn't have a clue what he wanted from me. And once, a sentry that was sitting downstairs called me-- I can hear my voice-- called me and said, Syda, there is a man downstairs wants to talk to you. I said, a man downstairs? Went down, that's the man. Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Pole with a-- some foreign citizenship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, who are you? What do you want with me? Why do you bring me all those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I watch you every day. You look such a nice girl, so different. And I said, in what way? I am no different. I'm Jewish like all the rest. And he said, there's something about you. And I said, so? I can't give you anything now. I'm-- I'm a nobody, proud of my religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, look, I have got a feeling, and I have got acquaintances. And I know what's going to happen to the-- unfortunately, going to happen to the Jewish people. And I thought I can use my foreign citizenship to take you with me. I will marry you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, marry me? And I called my husband, too, to be present. I said, marry me? And he said, yes, and we'll go overseas. And you will live a normal life. And I said, that is very, very nice of you, and very sweet of you. And I do appreciate it. I really do. I'll never forget it-- forget the offer. But how can I lead a normal life knowing what the Jewish people, my people, are going through? I belong with them. What happens to them will happen to me. We'll break for the end of the tape.
